---
title: "Chapter 36 — ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 35 · 🛒 Recommender Systems](ch35.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [37 · 🚨 Anomaly Detection →](ch37.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Time series analysis is the branch of machine learning concerned with data points stamped and ordered in time — stock prices, server load, daily sales, sensor readings, hospital admissions. What makes it special is that order carries information: yesterday tells you something about today, and you are usually trying to predict the future from the past. This chapter sits in the Applied ML Systems part of the field because forecasting is one of the most widely deployed practical uses of ML, and because it demands its own evaluation discipline that ordinary supervised learning gets wrong.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Applied ML Systems · forecasting and understanding time-ordered data · the one key idea is that observations are *dependent on their own past*, so you model the sequence, never shuffle it.
:::

## 36.1 — The anatomy of a time series: trend, seasonality, cycle, noise

Before modeling anything, it helps to see a time series as a sum (or product) of a few distinct ingredients. The intuition is the same as splitting a song into bass, melody, and background hiss: each part has its own behavior, and once you separate them, each becomes easy to handle.

A classical decomposition writes an observed value $y_t$ at time $t$ as

$$y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \varepsilon_t$$

where each term is a named **component**:

- **Trend** $T_t$ — the slow, long-run drift, up or down. Sales growing year over year.
- **Seasonality** $S_t$ — a pattern that repeats on a *fixed, known* period: higher ice-cream sales every summer, more traffic every weekday morning. The period is calendar-locked (7 days, 12 months).
- **Cycle** $C_t$ — a wave-like rise and fall with *no fixed period*, like business-cycle booms and busts. The key contrast with seasonality is that its length is irregular and unknown in advance.
- **Noise** $\varepsilon_t$ — the irreducible randomness left over once the structure is removed.

When the seasonal swing grows as the level grows (December sales spikes get bigger every year), the pieces *multiply* instead of add: $y_t = T_t \times S_t \times \varepsilon_t$. Taking a logarithm turns that back into an additive model, since $\log(T_t \times S_t \times \varepsilon_t) = \log T_t + \log S_t + \log \varepsilon_t$ — which is why log-transforming is such a common first move.

Here is the shape of the idea, with the messy observed line on top splitting into three clean pieces below:

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="9">
  <text x="4" y="12" fill="currentColor">observed</text>
  <path d="M10,30 Q40,18 70,26 T130,20 T190,30 T250,16 T310,26 T380,12" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="200" y="50" fill="currentColor" text-anchor="middle">=  trend  +  seasonality  +  noise</text>
  <text x="4" y="78" fill="currentColor">trend</text>
  <path d="M10,95 L380,70" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="4" y="125" fill="currentColor">season</text>
  <path d="M10,140 q20,-15 40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0 t40,0" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="4" y="172" fill="currentColor">noise</text>
  <path d="M10,185 l15,-6 l12,9 l14,-7 l13,8 l15,-9 l12,7 l16,-5 l13,6 l14,-8 l15,7 l12,-6 l16,8 l13,-7 l14,6 l15,-5 l12,7 l16,-6 l13,5" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
</svg>

Separating these pieces is **decomposition**. A simple, robust version is **STL** (Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess): estimate the trend with a smoother, average the de-trended values within each season slot to get the seasonal shape, and call the remainder noise. You decompose for two reasons: to *understand* (how much of last month's jump was just seasonality?) and to *forecast* each component separately, then recombine.

::: {.callout-tip}
Rule of thumb: if a plot of your series shows the up-and-down swings getting *wider* over time, model the log of the series, not the raw series. Multiplicative structure becomes additive, and most classical methods assume additive.
:::

## 36.2 — Stationarity and differencing

Most classical forecasting methods rest on one quiet assumption: the series is **stationary** — its statistical behavior does not change over time. Formally (weak stationarity), the mean is constant, the variance is constant, and the covariance between $y_t$ and $y_{t-k}$ depends only on the lag $k$, not on *when* you look. The intuition: a stationary series has no preferred direction and no clock; a snapshot from 2010 is statistically interchangeable with one from 2024.

Why does this matter? If the rules of the game keep shifting, the past is a poor guide to the future, and the math behind models like ARIMA simply breaks. So the standard workflow is: take a non-stationary series, *transform it* into a stationary one, model that, then undo the transform on your forecasts.

The workhorse transform is **differencing** — replace each value by its change from the previous step:

$$\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$$

A series with a linear trend becomes flat (stationary) after one difference; a quadratic trend needs two. For seasonal structure you take a **seasonal difference** at the period $m$, subtracting the value from one season ago: $y_t - y_{t-m}$. Differencing is the discrete cousin of taking a derivative — it strips out the level and exposes the dynamics.

```python
import numpy as np
# first difference removes a linear trend; seasonal difference removes a fixed-period pattern
y = np.array([10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20], float)
d1 = np.diff(y)                 # [2,2,2,2,2]  -> constant, now stationary
assert np.allclose(d1, 2.0)     # trend gone; mean is now flat
```

How do you know when to stop differencing? Plot it, and run a formal **unit-root test** like the **Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF)** test: its null hypothesis is "non-stationary," so a small p-value is your green light to treat the series as stationary. The common pitfall is **over-differencing** — differencing a series that is already stationary injects extra noise and negative autocorrelation, making forecasts *worse*. Difference the minimum number of times needed, not one more.

::: {.callout-warning}
Stationarity is not the same as "no pattern." A series can have strong, learnable autocorrelation and still be perfectly stationary — that structure is exactly what AR and MA models feed on. What stationarity forbids is the *statistics themselves* (mean, variance) drifting over time. Difference to kill the drift, not the signal.
:::

## 36.3 — Autocorrelation: ACF and PACF

Since a time series is correlated with its own past, the natural question is: *how far back does the memory reach, and in what shape?* Two diagnostic functions answer this, and reading them is the single most useful skill for choosing a classical model.

The **autocorrelation function (ACF)** measures the correlation between the series and a lagged copy of itself:

$$\rho_k = \frac{\text{Cov}(y_t,\, y_{t-k})}{\text{Var}(y_t)}$$

$\rho_k$ at lag $k$ answers "how similar is today to the value $k$ steps ago?" A slow, gradual decay in the ACF signals a trend or strong persistence; a spike every 12 lags screams yearly seasonality in monthly data.

The catch is that the ACF is *contaminated by chains*: if today correlates with yesterday and yesterday with the day before, then today correlates with two days ago purely by transitivity, even if there's no direct link. The **partial autocorrelation function (PACF)** removes that — it measures the correlation between $y_t$ and $y_{t-k}$ *after controlling for* all the lags in between, isolating the direct relationship.

```python
import numpy as np
def acf(y, k):
    y = y - y.mean()
    return (y[k:] @ y[:-k]) / (y @ y)   # lag-k autocorrelation, simple estimator
x = np.sin(np.arange(60) * 2*np.pi/12)  # period-12 seasonal wave
assert acf(x, 12) > 0.8                  # strong correlation at the seasonal lag
assert abs(acf(x, 6)) < 0.3              # half a period out -> near zero
```

The two plots together fingerprint a model. The classic reading:

| Pattern | ACF | PACF | Suggests |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory in the *shocks* | cuts off after lag $q$ | tails off slowly | MA($q$) |
| Memory in the *values* | tails off slowly | cuts off after lag $p$ | AR($p$) |
| Both | tails off | tails off | ARMA($p,q$) |

"Cuts off" means drops abruptly to near zero past some lag; "tails off" means decays gradually. This table is why people stare at correlograms (the bar-chart plots of ACF and PACF against lag) before fitting ARIMA.

## 36.4 — Classical models: moving average, exponential smoothing, ARIMA/SARIMA

The classical toolkit is small, interpretable, and still a brutally strong baseline — beat these before reaching for anything deep.

**Simple moving average.** The most basic forecast: average the last $w$ observations. It smooths noise but lags behind trends and treats a point from $w$ steps ago as exactly as important as yesterday's — rarely true.

**Exponential smoothing.** The fix is to weight recent points more, with weights that decay geometrically into the past. Simple exponential smoothing keeps one running level:

$$\ell_t = \alpha\, y_t + (1-\alpha)\,\ell_{t-1}, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

The smoothing factor $\alpha$ tunes memory: near 1 reacts fast and forgets quickly, near 0 is sluggish and stable. **Holt's** method adds a second equation for the trend; **Holt-Winters** adds a third for seasonality — three coupled smoothers, one each for level, slope, and seasonal shape. Holt-Winters is the go-to when you have clear trend *and* seasonality and want something fast and explainable.

```python
import numpy as np
def ses(y, alpha):
    l = y[0]
    for t in range(1, len(y)):
        l = alpha*y[t] + (1-alpha)*l   # blend new obs with running level
    return l                            # forecast = last level, flat into the future
y = np.array([10, 11, 9, 12, 13, 12, 14], float)
assert 11 < ses(y, 0.4) < 15            # tracks the upward drift, smoothed
```

**ARIMA.** The most famous classical model, **ARIMA(p, d, q)**, bolts three ideas together:

- **AR(p)** — *autoregression*: regress the value on its own $p$ past values, $y_t = \sum_{i=1}^p \phi_i y_{t-i} + \varepsilon_t$.
- **I(d)** — *integrated*: difference the series $d$ times first to make it stationary (Section 36.2).
- **MA(q)** — *moving average of errors*: let the forecast depend on the last $q$ prediction *errors*, $\sum_{j=1}^q \theta_j \varepsilon_{t-j}$.

You pick $p$ and $q$ from the PACF and ACF, and $d$ from how many differences achieve stationarity. **SARIMA** extends this with a second seasonal triple $(P, D, Q)_m$ that applies the same AR/I/MA machinery at the seasonal lag $m$ — handling, say, both day-to-day and week-to-week structure at once.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Raw series] --> B{Stationary?}
  B -- no --> C[Difference d times]
  C --> B
  B -- yes --> D[Read ACF and PACF]
  D --> E[Choose p, q]
  E --> F[Fit ARIMA p,d,q]
  F --> G[Check residuals look like noise]
  G -- structure left --> D
  G -- white noise --> H[Forecast]
```

A modern shortcut worth knowing: **auto-ARIMA** routines search over $(p,d,q)(P,D,Q)_m$ automatically, scoring each candidate with an information criterion (AIC/BIC, which reward fit but penalize extra parameters) and keeping the best. Convenient, but it does not free you from sanity-checking the residuals.

The pitfall with all classical models is assuming a single global structure: they expect the same dynamics throughout, struggle with abrupt regime changes, and (except via tricks) handle only one series and few external drivers. That gap is exactly what the next sections fill.

## 36.5 — Feature-based ML: turn forecasting into supervised regression

Here is the lazy, powerful move that dominates real-world tabular forecasting: stop using special time-series models and *reshape the problem into ordinary regression*, then throw gradient boosting at it. The idea is to build a feature table where each row is "what we knew at time $t$" and the label is $y_t$.

The core trick is **lag features** — past values used as columns:

$$\hat y_t = f(y_{t-1},\, y_{t-2},\, \dots,\, y_{t-k},\ \text{calendar},\ \text{exogenous})$$

To this you add **window features** (rolling mean / std / min / max over the last $w$ steps, which summarize recent level and volatility), **calendar features** (day-of-week, month, holiday flags — these capture seasonality *explicitly* instead of implicitly), and any **exogenous** drivers you have, like price or weather.

```python
import numpy as np
def make_supervised(y, k=3):
    # row t = [y_{t-1..t-k}], label = y_t   -> a plain regression table
    X, target = [], []
    for t in range(k, len(y)):
        X.append(y[t-k:t][::-1])   # most-recent-first lag window
        target.append(y[t])
    return np.array(X), np.array(target)
y = np.arange(1, 8, dtype=float)   # 1..7
X, target = make_supervised(y, 3)
assert X[0].tolist() == [3, 2, 1] and target[0] == 4   # predict 4 from {3,2,1}
```

Once the table exists, a **gradient-boosted tree** (XGBoost, LightGBM — see Chapter 08) is usually the strongest off-the-shelf forecaster for messy business data, because it captures nonlinear interactions, ignores irrelevant features, and needs little tuning. It is the default that wins many forecasting competitions for tabular series with rich covariates.

One real limitation: a tree can only *interpolate* between values it saw in training, so it cannot extrapolate a rising trend past the highest level in its history. The standard fix is to let the trees model the *de-trended, differenced* series (predict the change, not the level) and add the trend back afterward — another reason differencing from Section 36.2 keeps paying off.

::: {.callout-warning}
Two traps when building the feature table. First, **leakage**: a rolling feature for time $t$ must use only data strictly before $t$ — compute it on a shifted window, never including $y_t$ itself. Second, **multi-step forecasting**: trees predict one step. For longer horizons you either feed predictions back as inputs (recursive, errors compound) or train a separate model per horizon (direct, more models). Pick deliberately.
:::

## 36.6 — Deep learning and probabilistic forecasting

When you have many related series and long, nonlinear patterns, deep models earn their keep. The progression mirrors the rest of deep learning, just applied along the time axis.

**Recurrent nets (RNN/LSTM/GRU).** A recurrent network reads the sequence one step at a time, carrying a hidden state that acts as memory — the LSTM's gates let it hold information over long gaps. Natural for sequences, but slow to train and prone to forgetting very long contexts. (Mechanics live in Chapter 17.)

**Temporal convolutions (TCNs).** Slide 1-D convolutional filters over time, using *causal* convolutions (each output sees only the past, never the future) and *dilations* (filters that skip steps) to reach far back cheaply. They train fast in parallel and often match LSTMs.

**Transformers for forecasting.** Attention (Chapter 18) lets the model look directly at any past step, which is great for long-range and multivariate structure. Forecasting-specific variants (Informer, Autoformer, PatchTST, and time-series foundation models) tame attention's cost and add seasonal/trend decomposition inside the architecture.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  A[How much data?] --> B{One short series?}
  B -- yes --> C[ARIMA / Holt-Winters / Prophet]
  B -- no --> D{Rich covariates, tabular?}
  D -- yes --> E[Gradient-boosted trees]
  D -- no --> F{Many series, long nonlinear patterns?}
  F -- yes --> G[LSTM / TCN / Transformer / DeepAR]
  F -- no --> C
```

Two named tools you will meet constantly:

- **Prophet** — a decomposition model ($y_t = g_t + s_t + h_t + \varepsilon_t$: trend + seasonality via Fourier terms + holidays) fit in a Bayesian way. Not deep, but beloved because it is robust, handles missing data and holidays, and needs almost no tuning. A great strong baseline.
- **DeepAR** — trains *one* RNN across thousands of related series (all your products, all your stores), so each series borrows statistical strength from the others. Crucially, it outputs a **probability distribution**, not a single number.

That last point is the deepest idea in modern forecasting. A point forecast says "demand will be 100." A **probabilistic forecast** says "there's a 90% chance demand is between 80 and 130" — it predicts a distribution or a set of **quantiles** (cut-points like the 10th, 50th, and 90th percentile of the predicted outcome). For decisions (how much inventory to stock, how many nurses to schedule) the uncertainty *is* the answer; a number alone hides the risk. Probabilistic models are scored with the **pinball (quantile) loss**, which penalizes over- and under-prediction asymmetrically so each quantile lands where it should:

$$L_\tau(y, \hat y) = \max\big(\tau\,(y - \hat y),\ (\tau - 1)\,(y - \hat y)\big)$$

for a target quantile level $\tau$ (e.g. $\tau = 0.9$). When $\tau = 0.5$ this reduces to plain absolute error; pushing $\tau$ toward 1 makes under-prediction far more expensive than over-prediction, which is exactly what forces the 90th-percentile forecast to sit high enough.

**Multivariate** forecasting predicts several interacting series jointly (electricity demand across regions), letting the model exploit cross-series correlations a univariate model would miss. The honest caution: deep models need lots of data and careful validation, and on a single short series a tuned SARIMA or LightGBM will usually beat them. Reach for deep learning when you have *many* series or rich, long, nonlinear structure — not by default.

## 36.7 — Evaluation and time-series cross-validation

Everything above can be undone by one mistake: evaluating a time-series model the way you'd evaluate a normal classifier. The cardinal rule is **never shuffle, never let the future leak into training.** Random k-fold cross-validation trains on future data to predict the past, giving a fantasy score that collapses in production.

The correct protocol is **rolling-origin** (a.k.a. walk-forward / expanding-window) validation: always train on a contiguous past block and test on the block that comes *right after*, then roll the cutoff forward and repeat. The test set is always in the model's future, exactly as deployment will be.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TB
  subgraph folds["Walk-forward folds (time increases downward)"]
    direction TB
    F1["fold 1: train on early block, test on next block"]
    F2["fold 2: extend train, test on the block after"]
    F3["fold 3: extend train again, test on the block after"]
  end
  F1 --> F2 --> F3
```

For the metrics themselves, you compare forecasts $\hat y_t$ to actuals $y_t$:

| Metric | Formula (mean over horizon) | Reads as | Watch out |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{n}\sum \lvert y_t-\hat y_t\rvert$ | average error, same units | robust, but scale-bound |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_t-\hat y_t)^2}$ | error, penalizes big misses | dominated by outliers |
| **MAPE** | $\frac{100}{n}\sum\left\lvert\frac{y_t-\hat y_t}{y_t}\right\rvert$ | average % error | explodes when $y_t\approx 0$ |

MAE and RMSE are in the data's own units, so they can't be compared across series of different scale; MAPE is scale-free and intuitive ("8% off") but blows up or divides by zero when actuals are near zero — **sMAPE** (a symmetric version) and **MASE** (error scaled against the naive forecast's error) were invented to patch exactly that. Always anchor the number against a **naive baseline**: the forecast $\hat y_t = y_{t-1}$ ("tomorrow equals today"), or its seasonal version $\hat y_t = y_{t-m}$ ("same as last week"). A model that can't beat "same as last week" is not a model.

::: {.callout-warning}
A common, costly mistake: scaling or imputing using statistics from the whole dataset *before* splitting. The mean you subtract, the scaler you fit, the missing values you fill — all must be computed on the training block only, then applied forward. Fit on the past, transform the future; anything else leaks.
:::

## 36.x — Key takeaways

- A time series decomposes into **trend, seasonality, cycle, and noise**; seasonality has a fixed known period, a cycle does not. Log-transform when swings grow with the level.
- Most classical methods need **stationarity**; achieve it with the *minimum* **differencing** (check with ADF), and never over-difference. Stationary does not mean patternless — it means the statistics don't drift.
- Read the **ACF/PACF** correlograms to choose model orders: ACF cutting off ⇒ MA, PACF cutting off ⇒ AR.
- Classical models — **exponential smoothing / Holt-Winters** and **ARIMA/SARIMA** (and auto-ARIMA) — are strong, interpretable baselines you should beat before going fancy.
- Reframing forecasting as **supervised regression with lag, window, and calendar features** plus **gradient boosting** is the default winner for rich tabular series — guard against leakage, remember trees can't extrapolate, and decide recursive vs. direct multi-step.
- **Deep models** (LSTM, TCN, Transformers, DeepAR) and **Prophet** shine with many series and long nonlinear structure; **probabilistic / quantile** forecasts (scored by pinball loss) give the uncertainty that decisions actually need.
- Evaluate with **walk-forward (rolling-origin) validation — never shuffle**; report MAE/RMSE/MAPE against a **naive baseline**, and fit all transforms on the past only.

## 36.y — See also

- **Chapter 08 — Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning** — the gradient boosting that powers feature-based forecasting.
- **Chapter 17 — Recurrent & Sequence Models** — RNN/LSTM/GRU mechanics used by DeepAR and seq2seq forecasters.
- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — the backbone of modern long-horizon forecasting models.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — the general validation theory that walk-forward CV specializes.
- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** — lag/window/calendar features and leakage-safe scaling.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** — the probabilistic view behind Prophet and quantile forecasting.
- **Chapter 37 — Anomaly Detection** — spotting outliers in the same time-ordered data.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 35 · 🛒 Recommender Systems](ch35.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [37 · 🚨 Anomaly Detection →](ch37.html)
